title: Introduction to Cognitive Psychology
subtitle: Core theories of memory, attention, and decision-making
description: An eight-lesson academic overview of the foundational experiments and theoretical models that shape the modern study of cognition.
targetAudience: Undergraduate students in psychology, philosophy, or neuroscience
objectives: Define the cognitive revolution and its break from behaviourism
Describe the multi-store and working memory models
Distinguish selective, divided, and sustained attention

modules.title: Foundations
modules.description: The shift from stimulus–response to information processing.

lessons.title: The Cognitive Revolution
lessons.description: Why behaviourism collapsed as an explanatory framework.
lessons.content: The behaviourist program — study only observable stimulus and response — proved unable to account for language acquisition, planning, or mental imagery. Chomsky's 1959 review of Skinner's Verbal Behavior is widely cited as the inflection point.
keyPoints: Behaviourism excluded internal states by methodological fiat
Chomsky's poverty-of-stimulus argument undermined the core claim
The computer metaphor offered a tractable alternative

lessons.title: Information Processing
lessons.description: Inputs, encoding, storage, retrieval, and output.
lessons.content: Cognition can be decomposed into stages where information is transformed: encoded from perception, maintained in limited-capacity stores, operated on by rules, and used to produce behaviour.
keyPoints: Stage models are descriptive, not neurological maps
Capacity limits at each stage constrain overall performance
Reaction-time paradigms infer stage duration

lessons.title: Methods
lessons.content: Chronometric experiments, eye-tracking, dual-task paradigms, and neuroimaging each constrain different aspects of cognitive theory — no single method is decisive.
keyPoints: Reaction time is a function of encoding + comparison + response
Dual-task interference localises capacity limits
fMRI offers spatial resolution but low temporal resolution

quiz.question: Which methodological restriction did the cognitive revolution explicitly reject?
options: Exclusion of unobservable mental states from theory
quiz.explanation: Behaviourism's core methodological claim was that only observable behaviour could be legitimate subject matter; cognitive psychology re-admitted internal states as theoretical constructs.

modules.title: Memory
modules.description: Models of short-term and long-term storage.
objectives: Distinguish sensory, short-term, and long-term stores

lessons.title: The Atkinson–Shiffrin Model
lessons.description: The classic multi-store architecture of memory.
lessons.content: The 1968 multi-store model proposes sensory registers, a short-term store of limited capacity and duration, and a long-term store of effectively unlimited capacity. Transfer depends on rehearsal.
keyPoints: Sensory registers are modality-specific and brief
Short-term store is capacity-limited (≈7±2 items)
Rehearsal is the proposed transfer mechanism

lessons.title: Working Memory
lessons.content: The working memory model replaces a single short-term store with a central executive managing two slave systems — the phonological loop and the visuospatial sketchpad — plus a later-added episodic buffer.
keyPoints: Dual-task interference evidence motivates separate subsystems
The phonological loop handles verbal and acoustic information
The episodic buffer integrates modalities with long-term memory

lessons.title: Levels of Processing
lessons.description: Why depth of encoding matters more than duration of rehearsal.
lessons.content: Craik and Lockhart argue that semantic encoding produces better retention than structural or phonological encoding, independent of rehearsal time — challenging the Atkinson–Shiffrin rehearsal account.
keyPoints: Semantic processing produces more durable traces
Depth is inferred from retrieval performance, not measured directly
The framework predicts encoding-specificity effects

lessons.title: Forgetting
lessons.content: Forgetting is rarely pure decay; it is better modelled as interference from competing traces or failure to reconstruct the retrieval context.
keyPoints: Proactive and retroactive interference have distinct signatures
Context-dependent memory effects implicate retrieval over storage
Amnesic dissociations separate encoding from retrieval failures

modules.title: Attention and Decision
objectives: Distinguish early and late selection theories

lessons.title: Attention
lessons.content: Broadbent's early-selection filter and Treisman's attenuation theory disagree about how far unattended input is processed. Dichotic listening and cocktail-party evidence favours attenuation over a strict filter.
keyPoints: Early selection blocks unattended input before semantic analysis
Late selection processes all input but selects at response
Neither extreme matches all empirical findings

lessons.title: Dual-Process Reasoning
lessons.content: Dual-process theories — most prominently Kahneman's — distinguish fast, associative System 1 responses from slow, rule-governed System 2 reasoning. The account explains systematic errors on tasks like the Wason selection and the Linda problem.
keyPoints: System 1 is fast, parallel, and affect-laden
System 2 is slow, serial, and effortful
The distinction is a functional division, not a neural one

quiz.question: In Baddeley's working memory model, which component is specifically responsible for integrating information across modalities?
quiz.explanation: The episodic buffer was added to the original model specifically to account for cross-modal integration and linkage to long-term memory.
